speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two of the interview with Nathan Krieger. The date is December 4th, 1995. You were telling me how your family-- you were hiding with your mother, your future wife, and your-- her parents. What was your future-- what was your wife's name? Please tell us. What was your wife's name before-- I mean, what--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tonia. Tonia Schon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Schon. And her parents, Mr. and Mrs. Schon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her parents, Abraham and Malka Schon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And you were in-- where were you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in hiding in the middle of nowhere. And one evening, that guy-- my sister's neighbor-- he said I can't bring you any more bread, because there is talk in the village that he is bringing food, and-- and Jews are hiding someplace. And they're going to go to the police and to the Germans and tell them about it. So he said you have to leave here, because they're going to come after you. And I am sorry, he said. I can't bring you any more food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I ask him a favor, if he could do me a favor and go to the Bochnia ghetto. And I told him where my brother's working. If you could get in touch with him. And I said, if you can get in touch with him, could you please go to any Jew, and bring me a letter written in Yiddish. That-- what we should do, if we could come to the ghetto. We didn't know what went on, if people are in the ghetto, or whatever. So the next evening, he came and he says, this is the last time I could bring you bread. And he brought me a letter from a Jew from Bochnia ghetto. And he said that everything is almost normal. That we come-- that we should come to the ghetto if we have no other choice, that we should come to the ghetto. So that the evening, we walked. It was I think 15 or 16 kilometers. But that time, my mother wasn't a young woman. But somehow we managed. How long did it take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A whole night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we came in early in the morning. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours of walking, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I don't remember exactly. But I remember we walked the whole night. And because we had to stop, because my mother was unable to walk. And we came into the ghetto. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get into--

speaker: interviewee
text: We smuggled into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you smuggle yourselves into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I really don't remember exactly how.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a wall around the ghetto in Bochnia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But somehow it wasn't-- it was very hard to get out, but I don't remember-- really don't remember exactly how.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a guard at the ghetto gate?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never went in through the gate. We always, you know, pulled away some boards or whatever, you know, where nobody watched; because there was a guard, and there were Polish police walking around the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, how large?

speaker: interviewer
text: How many streets were inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a big ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give an idea of how many people were in there? Were there hundreds, or were there thousands?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was always people coming and going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live when you went into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we-- we got a room-- really I'm trying to-- I-- I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you together with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you together with any of your sisters or brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who was with you in the ghetto, in the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: My youngest sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were young children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My brother, my older brother, my younger brother, and my younger two sisters. And my older sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which older-- which of your older sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mirel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mirel. And one sister, Chana, which was the second one. I had an uncle living in Bochnia. And he had no children, so he like adopted my sister as a child. While she was still a child she went to live with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, not a child. She was 16, 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she went to live with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: She went to live with him. She was always-- while we were living in Vizhnitz. She was in Bochnia with my-- with my uncle. He was a butcher, and he was my mother's brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Itzak. Itzak Beldengreen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Tonia with you in the same room?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, she was with her parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage for food? Did they give food out? Were there rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: You were always-- if you had money, you were able to buy some food. And they gave-- the people would work. Like Tonia worked and my sisters worked. They worked in the [INAUDIBLE] in the same factory, which they made uniforms for the SS. So they gave you coupons for some kind of food and everything. It was-- you were-- you were able to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She was an older person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have cooking facilities in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had, you know, there was in one house probably lived who knows-- I don't remember how many families. And I wasn't-- I was for a while in the ghetto. And then they picked up people to work. So they took me near Krakow was a place where they working, [INAUDIBLE] there. Rakowitz was the name. So I went there. I was there for a while, and then I went back to the ghetto. And they said it's tough over there, so I ran away.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I managed-- from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Rakowitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Back to Bochnia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had I had a friend in Poland which he was like the [NON-ENGLISH] was called. It was like the mayor of a small town. And again, the same thing. I used to help when we went to school together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yusik Kopropski. And we had one guy which got killed, a friend of ours, during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I begged him, he gave me his name with my picture. So I-- and I was young. I had a head of hair, blue eyes. I looked like a Gentile a little bit. And I was never afraid for the Germans, because I had so-- I had those papers as a-- as a Gentile.

speaker: interviewer
text: This friend was a non-Jew, was a Gentile?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was a Gentile.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jospeh Kopropski was a Gentile?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had, like, the piece of paper from him with my picture. And so I was-- I was only afraid of the Polish police, because they recognize right away. They-- they knew which one is a Gentile. But the Germans, if they stopped you, so you took it-- they ask you for-- for papers, you took out the paper, you showed them and they said danke schoen-- thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this friend whose name you took?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joseph.

speaker: interviewee
text: I should. I remember the guy which was the mayor [INAUDIBLE]. But I don't remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was Joseph Kaprovsky?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was Jewish or Gentile?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was a Gentile. A Jewish couldn't be a Bergmeister, like [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like mayor here. So it was a little bit easier for me, because I always tried to, wherever they took me to work, I escaped, and tried to come back to Bochnia, because my family was there, and my Tonia was there. Until--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you-- you tell us, tell us a little bit more. How long were you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the ghetto in and out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until what time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: About a year?

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- about a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during the time that you were in the ghetto, did you get involved in any underground activities? Very little. I had friends, who they were there, you know. They-- they never were hiding from me or anything. I knew all about it. What did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they're doing and everything--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I don't know exactly what they were doing, because as I have a picture over there with my leader for my organization, he was the one what signed the signature exactly like the chief from the Gestapo, the chief of the Gestapo from Bochnia. So he used to make out like permits to travel, you know. If you wanted to travel, you have to have a permit from the chief from the Gestapo. He used to false his signature exactly--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Yechiel Symchowicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: He could forge the signature of the Gestapo chief.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, exactly. And he was the leader from the underground with my friend [NON-ENGLISH], which we went to school together. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there were more people from that Zionist organization, but they weren't in the ghetto. They were outside of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were hidden in the forest. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] from-- from there, they used to you know, sabotage things and everything. But I don't know exactly you know, what they were doing. And then in 1943, they liquidated the Bochnia ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were still in the ghetto, were there any actions in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life was very stable during the time of the ghetto? Yeah, people worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did people die in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: Died? Natural death. Nobody starved, you know, from hunger. There was-- there was food in the ghetto. You were-- you were able to survive. Were there any people arrested in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know of. Not that I know of. And then in 1943, they started this talk there's gonna be another [NON-ENGLISH]. That means they're gonna liquidate the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they deport people from the ghetto before they liquidated the whole ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They usually used to come and take the young people to work, you know, to different-- the Gestapo gave an order; we need 100 people, 150 people, young people, to go to work in different places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did those people come back to the ghetto usually?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people were removed from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But no one that you were friendly with was removed.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they took us to a place which was 40, 50 kilometers from Bochnia, which they took all-- all the ghettos, Tarnow, Bochnia, [INAUDIBLE], and they called us to our place.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1943. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when in 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must be-- usually everything happened before the holidays, or during the holidays. Must have been September. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They always picked that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were combining ghettos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, no, they took us to one place which was called Szebnie. Szebnie. And from there, they took us to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Szebnie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight days or something. I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take you from Bochnia to Szebnie?

speaker: interviewee
text: By trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took all the people at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of trains did they take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they-- did people resist leaving Bochnia? Did people hide? You couldn't resist it. You resist, they shot you. You had to do-- you had to-- they told them to take you to work, to a different places, and this and that. And whoever resists, whoever didn't want to go, they shot them on the spot. And that's it. Did you see people being shot who didn't want to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody went. Whoever was able to hide went away-- if he was able, if he had money or whatever. And whoever didn't, had to do what-- what they-- what they told you to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you prepare for this trip to Szebnie? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: How do you prepare? They tell you take a-- take a suitcase or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you put in the suitcase?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever you had. You didn't have much. [CHUCKLING] Whatever you had, you took-- you took with you. And you went.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in a car on this visit, on this trip to Szebnie?

speaker: interviewee
text: As many as could go in standing. You couldn't-- you couldn't sit down.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a difficult journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Without food, without anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it last longer than a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't think so. I don't remember. I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody die on the trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got to Szebnie?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they keep you in a place in Szebnie during those eight days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They kept us in a place. And then they-- again the same thing. Came in the-- the wagons. They tell you to get in, take off the shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when they-- when you came to Szebnie, I'm sorry, I just want to clarify that--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there waiting, you know, for the transport to take us-- to take us to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know when you came to Szebnie that this was just waiting to go to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You never knew where you're going to-- they tell you you are taking to a different labor camp, you know. And-- and you never knew where you're going, what's going to happen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You had no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about Szebnie? Did you talk to people on the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't remember too much. I wasn't there too long, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you came-- when you were coming to Auschwitz, did you still have your luggage, your valise?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They took everything as you went out on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Szebnie they took this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Before you entered the-- the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Szebnie.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Szebnie.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They ask you even to take off the shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Szebnie, yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: You shouldn't be able to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Szebnie.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Szebnie.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that when they were taking you to the train in Auschwitz-- for Auschwitz, you went barefoot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They ask us to take off the shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to the shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just laying there in front of the railroad tracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Szebnie.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Szebnie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the valises?

speaker: interviewee
text: You had nothing. You had no valises there anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they take away the valise?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly. But people didn't have much. You traveled from-- from Bochnia there. And whatever you had with you. And I try to recall. We had to leave everything in Szebnie, whatever somebody had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody took it? Somebody collected it from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know. We went-- no, and they said just leave it outside. Outside, before you entered the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they say they would give it back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't think so. I don't know. It was such a horrible scene and everything. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Szebnie.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Szebnie.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was horrible?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we knew that something-- something is wrong, that something is coming to an end. But we didn't know exactly, you know, what. If they tell you to take off the shoes and they tell you leave everything here, so, what could you think of it? If you're going to a labor camp, you know, they don't tell-- tell you to take off the shoes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then was the journey to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many-- who from your family was with you on this trip in Szebnie?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my sisters. My older brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister? Which sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I came with my-- the younger sisters-- two younger sisters, one older sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mirel, still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Joseph?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my older brother and my younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother? Was your mother with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother wasn't there with us. She didn't come to Szebnie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- when we were told in Bochnia ghetto that it's going to be [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH] means that they're going to send you away. So I took my mother in a hiding place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside of Bochnia, outside of Wisnicz. The place where she was there before. And the Polish chief of police was a very nice guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wisnicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did remember his name. He was killed by the Russians when the Russian came in. And he saved so many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you took your mother to him? You brought your mother there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A neighbor went to the German police and told him that his neighbor is hiding a Jewish person. And that was the second day of Kislev in 1943. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] '42? '43? And they came there to look for her. But the guy, the Polish-- when the Germans-- the Germans never went without a Polish policeman. So he used to serve them drinks and everything. And he told his son, go and tell them there that we're going to come. He should-- whatever he is doing, whatever he does, he should clean up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came there and looked around in the house, and they couldn't find anything. And it was snow on the ground. And he had a bunker. In the back of his house was a forest, and he made a bunker for my mother. And they went out to look in the back door, and they saw footsteps. And they went, and they took my mother and they send her with the transport. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And [INAUDIBLE] after the war, I knew the day when they took, and they figured out this takes three days to go to the crematorium. So I keep the [INAUDIBLE] the second day of Kislev. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after the whole families, with everybody, we had a gathering after 1945, and the rabbis decided that the 17-day [INAUDIBLE]. We have to use that for the whole families, because at that time Wisnicz was judenrein. And they took everybody away. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The man who hid your mother was a police chief?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Was an ordinary-- [DRINKS] was an ordinary Gentile. Nice guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: The man who warned him was a Polish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was this chief of the police. He was a very, very nice man. He helped so many people, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: He did something for the Polish government in 1936 or '37. And when the communists came to power, the first thing they-- they remembered what he was doing at that time. And the guy risked his life through the war helping people. Such a nice person he was-- while we were in Wisnicz. We still had a [NON-ENGLISH] and used to slaughter, you know, for kosher meat and everything. And when they went to the police in town, you know. So he went there, so he used to send his son or his daughter on the bicycle and tell him clean up the room, you know, wash up all the blood, you know. Nobody should see it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean when the Germans were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was real. A real mensch. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then the Russian came, and they took him away. And that was the end of him. After the war, I was told.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything happen to the man who had hidden your mother in a bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't-- I really don't remember exactly--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because I never went back there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out what happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I knew about it, you know. The brought her into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see her before she was taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was the last time I saw her. [SOBBING] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not easy for me to live all those years. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Why did I survive? Out of nine children? [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] And then you were taken to Szebnie.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a guilty conscience. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Afterwards, when you were-- those of you who were there, your sister, your brother, when you went to Szebnie-- Szebnie. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Tell me about the trip from Szebnie to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the trip, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- it was more frightful than the trip from Bochnia.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very cold. It was-- it took us I think around three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was the crying, the screaming. And-- and they put in God knows how many people, you know. And those cattle wagons. And you had no room where to sit down. You-- you had-- it was horrible. You had no place where to go to urinate, or whatever, you know. And people was standing, and it was cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause now and take a tape change.
